speaker: interviewer
text: --with Henry Wermuth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wermuth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it is type number 11.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were in the camp called [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were hiding, resting behind the boxes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And one day I fell asleep. And suddenly, I heard noises. And I got a shock. But then I found there were two prisoners also having a rest there, sort of.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, when you sort of go away from the group for possibly either try-- saying, relieving yourself or something. You know? Just-- you could get away for a minute or two.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, they talked to each other. And I could hear their conversation, their hopes and their beliefs, more described in the book in detail. And I-- you know, when I woke up, I-- I bumped my head against the wooden box.

speaker: interviewer
text: And but I didn't hear it. I got a shock but I didn't hear it. And I thought, should I tell them something? You know, you better go away or something, not stay too long? Anyway, suddenly a German came-- came in and said, what are your dogs-- dogs doing here? And chased them out, hit them, hit them. And you could hear their screams, even in the distance.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the evening, we always-- not only did I have to walk uphill for 20 minutes, but also had to push a cart with-- on two wheels with our sort of shovels and you know, with tools. And at this time it was a bit harder to push because there were two people who couldn't walk anymore and were 1/2 or 3/4 dead. And I pushed up. And I wish that I had-- had warned them. Anyway, I stepped out of my barracks that evening and looked in other barracks nearby. And I-- through an open door, I could see a bathtub filled with water. People were standing around, looking. And the water was almost to the brim. And one of the other prisoners pushed those down under water. And they were too weak to fight back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I must have really been hardened. Because instead of feeling sorry for them, I felt-- the thought came to my mind, well, what else can one do with people in that state? Anyway, this camp was-- hold one of the worst memories for me. One day the work was finished. And I said, what are we going to do now? Whatever we put unnecessarily from place A to B, will they ask us to put it back, back from B to A? They said, half of you will only go out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, imagine, the first ones tried to move back to the back, and we-- when we had to get in line. And the other ones didn't want to allow that. And it was a-- the shuffling that was going on, great to the amusement of the kapos. But terrible otherwise. And then of course, with a few mighty swings of the-- whatever they had to hit us with-- we got in line. Anyway, I had to march out. My father-- at that time, I will call it lucky-- stayed back. And it wasn't so hard work anymore. Because there was really nothing to do. And the people were perhaps, even tired from beating up. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I came back home to a very rested father. He was really lucky. Or was he? The next time, they had a same performance. Everybody wanted to get in the rear.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as it happened in the end, I was the front again, and my father stayed back. I worked in evening. And I came home. I was looking for my father. I couldn't find him. I looked everywhere. The-- the camp was rather small. I would say, I'm not sure. I described in the book, more in detail. It was only a few hundred people, as I-- as I said. I looked everywhere, I couldn't find him. Even tried to sneak in the bathtub, if they had drowned her or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no. Where could he be? Suddenly, I found there was even like a-- a camp hospital there, in [INAUDIBLE]. A block where people lined up if they didn't feel well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went there. There was a queue in front of a doctor's door, behind which was a doctor and asked a description of the person, the patient inside. It wasn't my father either. And somewhat it was already, pretty dark, pointed into a corner of the waiting room where I could feel-- where I could find or could I see something on-- somebody on the-- something on the floor. And it was my father, sitting with his head on his knees, groaning. Anyway, he got-- he got bed rest by the doctor. And this very, very strong man who was much stronger than even I was, and I was quite strong because of-- well, I have told you the story in the camps-- a very strong man was beaten. I almost want to say, to a pulp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? I found out later, unfortunately, not in enough details as I would wish to tell you, the commandant came in and saw the people and said, what are the Jews doing here? And then what-- you know, they're not at work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he sent them down the mine. A kapo there who had never had anybody-- any Jewish people that sort of had a few victims. And I'm not sure if there were anybody else killed, or what happened. My father was one of those. And the only thing is I could say, I'm not going to allow him to be-- have the-- have the same fate as the others had in that bath. Anyway, he was in the bed. And after two or three days he could speak, already, normally. After six days or so he already started to walk. And a day later-- if he would have to go to work he would have been finished. And a day later, we are told we were going to be evacuated again. Now, he just has-- had recouped amazingly fast and could walk again. And so it-- he could have called one of those death marches and not because there were hardly any Jews there, any Jews left. And they were not used to Jews. And they were not used to employ guards that would kill. Although the strong ones-- at a column, marched faster and faster. We went slower and slower. And I had my father in arm-- in-- in-- you know, sort of at my-- on my right. And I was-- we were marching. The first half hour it was even a pleasure. The morning air and-- and it's downhill. And after an hour, we you know, started feeling already, now it's-- we wish you could rest. And after a little longer, I-- my legs were numb. And a little longer still, and I did not feel anything anymore. They were marching. I don't know how. The connection with the brain and the legs, that on earth, I don't know how it works. But I couldn't feel anything anymore. It was I had no body below my waist. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After a long while, we found the others were stopped for lunch or for some kind of something to eat. And we are breathing easier now, we have a little rest. But I couldn't sit down. I let myself drop onto sort of like a-- a little hill. And two minutes later, the command was given. March again. Oh no. I couldn't get up. I was held up. And somehow, I was up again. Again, by my father. And how he could stand it, I don't know. But the first ones must have reached the-- the destination long ago. And we were in at-- you know, at the very end. And those weak ones always tried to be a step ahead of us. Because the guard in the back made, already, noises of great impatience. And we were he might shoot. Because-- because you know, we were the last ones. And I heard him speak and I could imitate dialects, and his dialect then and try to imitate this. I said-- in a German dialect-- not long anymore, and we will be home by mother-- with mother. And after that, he kept-- he didn't say anything, what that we were so slow. On the contrary, when the other guards went fine, have a way, he started a conversation. I wouldn't have spoken about the conversation. But only because he came from a village, had never-- he knows what-- about Jews, but he had never known a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: He knew Frankfurt, came from, but he had never heard of Auschwitz. What should I argue with him, tell him anything? Anyway, night came. And again, there was a moment where I could have disappeared. Because left and right, there were sort of ditches.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we could have just strolled down and waited till they would pass by. But we didn't. Because the idea of getting, maybe, something to eat at night was too strong. A lorry came for us and took us to the destination. Now, I have to tell you something very important. At the start of the march, in the morning, half dark, there were several noise. And we heard a voice, calling, who is not strong enough to walk come onto the lorry. Now, we had seconds to decide. Our past experience was not to go, not to listen to that thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who knows when someone is too weak, where it will end? Gas chambers. But there were no gas chambers. That camp, that was too small. What will happen? But the moment it passed, to-- to the side, it was the first of the bad decisions. And more to come, later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, in the evening, some lorries took us the rest of the way. I was sitting down on the floor. And I remembered not knowing, not feeling, even, that I was sitting. But we were given hot soup and it was heaven, whatever it was. And we had to march a little bit longer. And then we had to stay the night in a barn. And in the barn, people separate themselves. The Germans-- so the Germans, others-- others, and the Jews took-- you know, it was big heaps of straw, where we could rest on. And in the morning, some German said his bread ration was stolen. Now, we couldn't possibly-- none of us could have got there. Besides, you would have heard any movement in the straw. But the camp eldest, with the name of Adolf Fernbacher, lined up the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course, only Jews could have done such a thing. Have you done it? He asked the first one. No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go away. Second one, no. Third one, no. I noticed we were only something between 20 and 30 left out of the 150. Now, I did something which, again, was against our-- be not conspicuous, whatever you do. I had my father in arm again. And I was the last. And hearing him say, no you weren't. No you weren't, go, go. I said, well, I have nothing to fear. I'm going to say. But before I said no, it wasn't me, he lifted what I was think-- a-- a piece of wood, like, size of a table leg. And he hit me on the head. He was very strong. And I was fast. And my hand got in between. But my father wasn't. And he broke down. And blood spurted from his head. He managed to crawl into the barn. The next thing I remember, we were in a train again. On this train journey there is, also, a story to tell. For the first two weeks, not much. Except that I looked, saw the hole in his head. And I even saw the piece of hair inside. I wished I had pulled it out. But because I once-- a classmate of mine had the same thing. And you know, he was cured afterwards anyway. And I sort of thought, well, he is indestructible. I had no other feeling, that this-- this man is indestructible. And then he started complaining. We were three weeks in a train-- train. And I started counting from a certain-- in my book, from a certain period, 12 days minus liberation. 11, 10, 9, 8, 7, and so on. And to the point-- and on the-- on the 12th day of our-- anyway, one of the end days-- I sort of have to sort of now recoup it again. I should look it up in my book. My father complained so much that he was taken to a nearby wagon, which was called, again, a Konkan Wagon, which like-- like a hospital wagon, which you could not have found, I'm sure, in other-- in any other train. I have to mention just one thing. And that is a switch back to when we landed in Mauthausen. People, when they open the trains, they was throwing themselves on the-- onto the snow. Many died for that. And from the 5,000 which were in the train, 2 and 1/2 thousand arrived. And what I was, at that time, thinking, envying that they had a roof over their head. And I had none in winter, was a blessing. Because we had contact with the-- with a man who gave us the hot water in the engine. And we had to eat and drink. And the others were just dying. 50%, 5 and 1/2-- from 5,000, 2 and 1/2 arrived. Now, switch back to this train. We were not so crowded, as I later learned other trains were. But crowded enough that at one time my strongest wish was to just stretch out my legs without touching anybody else. Because when I was-- when we were sitting it was all right. When we were sleeping other legs came on top of me. And my legs were on top of somebody else. And my father then had to go to this couple of doors-- wagons away, into what they call a-- a hospital wagon. And at the same time, there was an alarm. And the SS, you know, they closed us-- the-- the shutters in. And they were hiding, either under the train or ran into the nearby woods. It was also something I missed in Auschwitz. There was an alarm. And the only bomb fell not too far away from me, belying the-- the Allied-- the Allieds saying that Auschwitz couldn't be bombed. It was too far. But one bomb-- bomb-- bomb dropped there, you know, on the way to Germany. Come back to this. And it was, you know, a joy to see the SS running for their lives, the masters. And I only hoped that the bomb wouldn't kill me. Sort of-- that would be sort of ironical. And I'm sure that it might have happened some other places where they were killed by friendly bombs, friendly fire, so to say. Anyway, a nearby train was bombed. And people were let out too rummage-- except for the Jews-- rummage in that train, for anything they could use. Preferably something to eat. And I'm not sure if it was mockery that the next train, the Jews were allowed to go out. And we went to the train. And we looked and we looked. And I had a wagon to myself. There weren't many Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what could I find? I found an onion. I don't know if it's got any sulfur. I was afraid it might even hurt-- harm me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had, in the train, somebody from the-- from the day when people sort of went and got them self food, some raw potato came to me. And I ate it, bit by bit, the raw-- in the raw way. Anyway, this day I found that onion. And I was wondering if I could eat it. And I bit into it one bite. I put it in my pocket. And then we were called. A, man the SS guard wanted us to lift a blown-off roof of a train to find out if there's something to be found underneath.

speaker: interviewer
text: So 20, 30 of us lined up there, lifted the roof. There was nothing underneath. And as we lifted it, we had to go sort of-- for-- go forward with it because it was too tall.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought, oh my. If people let go, what happens. And then when there was nothing underneath, we went back. And some people had let go. But-- and the roof came crashing down. I think there were two people under that roof. But they could possibly be safe because two people were hit by it and were killed by it. And the end of the roof was leaning on it. So the middle, if others were in the middle, could possibly be saved. Because the roof was rounded in the middle. But I didn't know. Four victims. But no, there were five. But I was only hurt. Because as the roof came down, and again, it was a matter of escaping death by millimeters, centimeters, my arm-- you can hardly see it now, the scar. It is sort of full of blood, running blood. Anyway, we are marching home. And I registered-- registered with glee that we were not asked to march in line. And you know, like, going for a walk home. The guards couldn't care. I could see. But then when we ended, sort of the near, where we you know, surrounding of the-- of the train, the guards left us. We had to line up. And we were beset by another line of prisoners. I think they were Russians. Well, we are-- they were the low and we were the lowest. And they were searching us because they had a good result yesterday. And they were thinking maybe they'll find something. And as they were searching I could see my neighbor fidgeting. And he had, in his hand-- I don't know if he had more than that-- sort of a tin of something. It was dented badly in the middle. Instead of him handing it to-- out of fear-- to the others, I grabbed it. And I had my trousers in my boots, let it down inside my trousers. And it sort of wrapped itself around my ankles. And sort of, for the first gloves, it wouldn't be seen, like knickerbockers. And he searched everybody. And then one came to him. And not he-- a number-- a number of them, sort of they did it fast. And just in case, not wanting their comrades to find more than they did. They came to me, and I said, I have nothing. All I have is-- and I pull it out of my pocket, the onion. And it was, by the time-- it was then blood soaked, by that time. I lifted him-- in order for his glances to look up and not down, I lifted it over his head as he was searching my pockets already, before he went down any further. And a drop of blood fell on his face. And he cursed me and pushed me away. And I kept my bloody onion, or half onion, by that time. And as I went on to the train, the wagon-- the-- the hospital train was open.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was sit-- my father was sitting with the back, near the door, by was back, to the outside, on a-- on a stool. And a man was working on him, operating. And it was such a noise, the din from the people who-- who searched my comrades, that I could not understand what he was saying or rather, answering when I said the-- the funny-- asked a funny question, how-- how you're doing or something, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was absolutely unnecessary. But I wanted to have some kind of a conversation. But I could see sideways, like a smile. And I was like, reassured. And I went on, into my-- into my wagon. Got in my corner. And not many people inside the wagon. The weather was nice. And I managed, somehow, to open-- I don't know how, to open the tin. And it was meat, corn beef, fat. Something my body hasn't known for how-- how long. But I was very, very careful not to sling it down with one tiger sling. I sort of ate it little bit at a time. And I also wanted to save some for my father. And every now and again, an ever-shorter distance of time, if I may say that way, I had another nibble, another nibble. And then to the great shock, I found that I was nearly at the end. And instead of giving it to my father whom I-- maybe I could help him, the doors were closed. People came in. And later on, I couldn't pass it on to him anymore anyway. But I had great reproaches later on. Because the next day, somebody came into my-- opened the door in the morning and called my name. Now, I hadn't heard my name for a long time because I was a number. And I said, oy. And he said what I expected him to say. Your father is dead. Now, I remember one single tear-- that was all-- stole out of my eye. Before it was down, halfway down, it was dried. I remember it because-- I remembered it so many times later. And I will speak to it in a minute again, about that. Anyway, obviously, it came to me. I probably am alone now, but maybe father, mother, and sister are still alive somewhere. Because when you are there, you see living people. And then you see maybe, well, I was alive. Maybe they are as well, by some miracle. The next thing I remember, that was eight day-- eight days before the-- before my liberation, and 11 days before the war was declared officially at the end. And the-- that was one of the things that made me stop talking every time I started. And that is also a reason why I had to write a book. Because beyond this point, I could never speak. Because previously, I have repeated it now so often, I had a lump in my throat and I couldn't go on. Now, the next thing I remember was that I found myself. And it must have been very nearby, in Mauthausen. And somebody says, they don't gas here or well, I'll say at least that. Now, I'm not sure if gassed there or not. But at the time, when we arrived there, maybe they didn't gas anymore. And I would say that most likely-- I'm not sure at that stage or definitely a few days later, the top elements of the camp had left already. Wherever they went, to hiding places or not, I don't know.
